stories.title: The Lamplighter's Accord
stories.text:
Captain Merra of the Iron Coast has smuggled everything—plague-victims, forbidden scrolls, stolen shadows in crystal jars. Tonight, she carries something worse: a living contract.

The creature in her hold is no demon, no spirit. It is a merchant's broken oath given flesh by coastal magic—a pale thing with eyes like old coin, speaking in the voice of the man who swore and failed to pay. It demands passage to the Sapphire Deep, where the deepkin still remember the debt.

"I do not traffic in curses," Merra tells her crew, though her hands shake.

"You do now," the oath-thing whispers.

When her ship reaches the deep-blue waters, deepkin rise. They do not attack. Instead, they sing—a melody of accounting, of debts coming home. The creature dissolves into salt-foam, and the merchant's voice fades.

But Merra's shadow remains darker than before. The captain has gained passage for her cargo and safe harbor for a season. The price: she must deliver one more oath-thing to the deep waters before the year's end.

She sets sail at dawn, searching the Iron Coast's wind-haunted ports for the next broken promise. The sea remembers. And now so does she.

stories.title: The Record-Keeper's Shadow
stories.text: Zareth shelves ledgers in the Sovereign's Archives, his hands steady, his mind a blank page. He remembers nothing—not the ritual, not the peak, not the price. A customs auditor arrives with reports of missing caravans in the Scorched Reach, and Zareth, sorting the manifest, sees something that troubles him: three consecutive years of spice shipments, all perfectly accounted for, yet somehow the merchants arrive with less than they should carry. The numbers are true, but the ledger feels wrong. When he mentions this to a scribe, the woman's face goes pale. "Don't speak of that," she whispers. "The Reach has been closed since the Conjunction. Merchants who venture there now return... changed. Some don't return at all." Zareth files the observation and moves to the next stack. But later, when he shelves records in a forgotten corner, he finds a journal written in his own hand—pages filled with sketches of stars, maps of rituals, names of places he has never seen. His fingers shake. He does not remember writing this, but his heart tells him it matters. He closes the journal and returns it to the shelf, choosing not to ask questions. Some blanks, he senses, should remain unfilled.

stories.title: The Starborn Merchant
stories.text: In Vethrim's night markets, a cloaked woman called Vex moves through crowds with her characteristic whisper-soft gait, but something has shifted in her countenance. The new stars—birthed by the Stellar Rite—shine brighter than the old, and Vex finds herself drawn to their light in ways she cannot explain. Her memory is fragmented; she recalls fragments of another life, another purpose. A desperate client approaches: a nobleman's son, cursed to speak only truth, seeks magical relief. Vex quotes a price—steep, but fair—and the boy accepts. As she prepares the working, she pauses. Something in the quality of starlight troubles her, as though the world has been subtly rewritten without her knowledge. She completes the transaction and takes her payment in coins that feel lighter than they should. That night, she visits an old acquaintance and asks obliquely if anything unusual happened during the last conjunction. The acquaintance's smile is knowing. "Better not to know," he says. Vex nods and leaves, but a new unease settles over her—the sense that she has forgotten something profound, and that forgetting itself is the price she must forever bear.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Vigil
stories.text: In the Drowned Hollows, where the Decay-Lord's plague once swept through the living and dead alike, Kess of the Drowned Hollows tends to the graves. The septic rot has been purged, but the dead do not rest easily. New stars wheel overhead—bright, almost accusatory—and Kess feels them in her bones. As a bone-singer and curse-bearer, she senses what others cannot: the stellar magic has altered something fundamental in the relationship between the living and the dead. The corpses she tends no longer sing their old songs of despair. Instead, they hum a different tune—something ancient, something that predates kingdoms. Kess carves rune-marks into stone to honor the countless fallen, her hands moving with strange certainty, as though the bones themselves are guiding her work. The very ground seems to resonate with a new frequency, one that speaks of vast cosmic shifts. She realizes the dead now know something of the working that saved the world. Kess continues carving through the night, her cursed hands etching names of the fallen into stone that will outlast empires, forever preserving the memory of those the plague took.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Last Map
stories.text: Mardoc the Cartographer has spent years mapping the Bleached Wastes and the pre-human ruins hidden within them. His charts are meticulous, his knowledge invaluable to scholars and merchants alike. But since the Violet Conjunction, his maps have begun to shift. Not literally—the parchment remains still—but the territories he has recorded now feel imprecise, as though the stars themselves have redrawn the world's boundaries. He receives a commission from the Sovereign's office: chart the consequences of the Stellar Rite. Map the places where the new starlight has changed the land itself. Mardoc ventures into the Wastes with fresh instruments, and what he discovers troubles him. The ancient ruins have begun to glow faintly at night—not with bioluminescence, but with a light that seems to come from within the stone itself. The inscriptions he cannot read—languages that predate humanity—now appear to shimmer when the new stars are visible. A pattern emerges in his sketches: the ruins are arranged in a configuration that mirrors the new constellation. Whoever built these cities was anticipating this moment. Mardoc completes his maps and delivers them to the Sovereign, but he keeps one sketch for himself: a projection of what the ruins will become in a thousand years, as the new starlight slowly awakens their ancient purpose. He is not sure he wants to know if he is right.

stories.title: The Warlord's Last Stand
stories.text: Kael Stormbreaker rules his mountain fastness with an iron fist and a heart consumed by ambition. For decades, he has held his kingdom through sheer force of will and martial prowess, bending rival lords to his dominion through strategy and strength. But the new stars have changed something fundamental in the world's power structure. A rival warlord, sensing shifting currents, launches an assault on the mountain fortress. Kael commands his soldiers with masterful precision, yet something feels different in the air—as though the cosmic working that birthed the new stars has subtly altered the very nature of power itself. His usual advantages seem diminished. For the first time, victory does not come easily. Kael descends to the battlefield with his remaining soldiers, fighting not as an inevitable instrument of conquest, but as a man gambling with his dominion's future. He holds the gates long enough for his people to escape into the high passes, and as the enemy closes in, he laughs—a sound of genuine, hard-won defiance. In his final moments, watching the new stars wheel overhead, he wonders what the cosmic shift means for the world he leaves behind.

stories.title: The Seer's New Vision
stories.text: Isenna, seer of Keth'moris, has spent her life reading fragments and shadows, glimpsing possible futures through a veil of uncertainty and misdirection. Prophecy has never been her gift so much as her curse—visions that come unbidden, offer cryptic wisdom, and are as likely to mislead as enlighten. But on the night of the Conjunction, when the new stars blazed into existence, something changed in the nature of prophecy itself. She saw Zareth—though she did not know his name—standing blank-eyed in an archive, shuffling papers he could not remember writing. She saw a thousand years spiraling forward, stars burning, darkness encroaching, and a debt unpaid. She felt the weight of a sacrifice so vast it bent the fabric of possibility itself. Every night, Isenna dreams of the new stars and what their birth has wrought. A merchant approaches her seeking a reading about trade routes, and when she touches his palm, she sees caravans vanishing into the Scorched Reach, sees merchants transformed into something neither living nor dead, sees a hunger awakening in the bleached lands. "Do not travel east," she tells him. "Something is coming through the old places." The merchant leaves angry, believing her mad. But Isenna knows better. The stars have granted true prophecy—and true prophecy is a burden almost unbearable.

stories.title: The Young Sorcerer's Inheritance
stories.text: Deep in the Blasted Lands, the Young Sorcerer emerges from the Spire to find the world transformed. The stars are new. The Stellar Rite—the great cosmic working that the Elder Mage (now dead) would have witnessed through the lens of this heir—has been performed. The Young Sorcerer, who inherited their master's knowledge but not their power, feels the change immediately. Wards that were ancient and inviolable now flicker with uncertainty. The lines of power that flow through the world—visible to those with eyes to see—have been redrawn. The Young Sorcerer realizes, with a mixture of awe and resentment, that someone has performed the impossible: a working so vast it has altered the structure of magic itself. They descend into the Spire's depths, where the Elder Mage's Codex is kept, seeking answers. In those pages, written by a master now dead, they find a reference to the Stellar Rite—and a grim assessment of what it would cost. "If the stars are to be saved," the Elder Mage wrote, "the price will be paid by one who cannot afford it. The working will demand memory, joy, self—everything that makes a person human. Whoever performs it will live, but they will live as a blank page, forever unable to know what they have accomplished." The Young Sorcerer closes the codex, understanding for the first time the full scope of what has been done. They do not know who paid the price, but they feel the weight of it—the way it has reshuffled every magical current in the world. The Young Sorcerer descends deeper into the Spire, knowing that the age of their master is truly ended, and they must learn to navigate a world rewritten.

stories.title: The Drowned Prince's Vigil
stories.text: Sylas the Deepwarden of the deepkin dwells in the Cerulean Trench, the deepest place in the known oceans, where the water tastes of centuries and pressure shapes thought. Since the Violet Conjunction, something has changed in the fundamental nature of the deep places. The new stars, though impossibly distant, send their light down into the trench, and Sylas—a warden of the deepkin and a being ancient beyond mortal reckoning—feels the cosmic disturbance like a wound in the water itself. The deepkin have their own prophecies, older and more reliable than human seers, and those prophecies spoke of a day when the stars would fall and be reborn. That day has come and gone. Sylas gathers a council of elder deepkin to discuss what this means. The consensus is unsettling: the balance between the surface world and the deep has been disturbed. Something has been given away to save the stars—a debt has been paid in a currency the deepkin only half understand. Trade with surface merchants is becoming difficult; some refuse to venture into the deep waters, speaking of strange currents and lights that should not exist. Sylas suspects that the reshuffling of cosmic magic has opened pathways long closed between the deep places and the world above. She sends scouts to the boundary waters to investigate, but none return with clear answers. Instead, they speak of a humming in the water itself, as though the deep is learning to sing a new song. Sylas settles into her vigil, waiting to understand what the new stars have brought to her realm.

stories.title: The Blackthorn Holdfast's Shadow
stories.text: Ashara the Archer, legendary for her forty-day siege at Thornholt, now finds herself in the quieter role of fortress keeper—a position that suits her better than she expected. But quietude is ending. Refugees have begun arriving from the Scorched Reach and the Ashen Wastes, fleeing something they cannot name. Some are half-mad with terror; others arrive changed in ways that are difficult to articulate. Ashara tends to her fortress and listens to their stories. They speak of merchants who ventured into the Reach and returned wrong—still walking, still speaking, but missing something essential. They speak of the light from the new stars affecting the desert differently than it affects other lands, as though the Wastes are responding to the cosmic change. Ashara, practical and unafraid, begins fortifying her hold. She has withstood siege before; she can withstand whatever approaches from the bleached lands. But as she works, she finds herself studying the new constellations each night, wondering if they hold some hidden meaning. An old ranger, passing through on her way south, stops at the fortress and asks to speak with Ashara privately. "The Wastes are waking," the ranger says. "The new stars have kindled something ancient in the desert stones. Within a year, perhaps two, the boundaries between the deep Wastes and the civilized lands will begin to blur. You should prepare." After the ranger departs, Ashara stands alone on the highest tower of Blackthorn and studies the distant Wastes. Somewhere out there, something is stirring. She has faced enemies with swords and arrows; she wonders what weapons will be needed for what comes next.
